paper_title: Constraints to Sustained and Inclusive Growth in Sri Lanka: An Initial Diagnosis and Key Topics for Discussion
research_answer: Sri Lanka holds an impressive 28% global share of tea exports — the largest of any country — but the competitive dynamics of tea are problematic for Sri Lanka's economic trajectory. Its main competitors are countries with dramatically lower income levels: Kenya at $1,300 GDP per capita and India at $1,600, compared to Sri Lanka's $3,800. This means Sri Lanka is competing in a labor-intensive commodity against countries that have a structural cost advantage. With tea making up 12% of Sri Lanka's export basket, continued dependence on this product at its current income level raises serious questions about long-run cost competitiveness, since wages in Sri Lanka are naturally higher than those of its key rivals.
supporting_quotes: Tea: Low income competitors... Global Share of Tea Exports (Net) in 2014 [showing Sri Lanka 28%, China 24%, India 14%, Kenya 15%] and GDP per capita showing Sri Lanka at ~$3,800, Kenya at ~$1,300, India at ~$1,600
